definition: Widely deployed and well understood in libraries or adjacent industries
Increasing adoption but still evolving in practice
Limited deployment, pilot projects, or active research area
AI may produce incorrect, incomplete, or misleading results.
AI may fabricate facts, sources, citations, or metadata.
AI may reinforce historical, cultural, demographic, or algorithmic biases.
Patron, staff, or institutional data may be collected, analyzed, or exposed.
Users may not understand how recommendations or decisions were produced.
The extent to which library services, interfaces, and outputs can be used by people with disabilities and diverse access needs.
Questions about ownership, licensing, training data, or reuse of content.
Communities, viewpoints, or identities may be omitted or misrepresented.
Unclear responsibility for AI-assisted decisions or outputs.
Performance may be inconsistent across contexts or over time.
Poor, incomplete, outdated, or inconsistent input data may affect outcomes.
Results require professional review and should not be accepted automatically.
Users may need justification or reasoning behind recommendations.
Services may not benefit all users equally.
Potential legal, regulatory, policy, or ethical compliance issues.
A Large Language Model trained on vast amounts of text to understand and generate human language, answer questions, summarize information, and perform other language-related tasks.
Technology that creates new content such as text, images, audio, code, metadata, or summaries based on patterns learned from training data.
AI systems capable of processing and generating multiple forms of content, such as text, images, audio, and video.
The practice of designing and refining prompts to improve the quality, accuracy, and usefulness of AI-generated outputs.
AI systems that can plan, make decisions, and perform multi-step tasks with varying levels of autonomy.
Structured processes in which AI agents execute sequences of tasks, often combining reasoning, retrieval, and external tools.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines information retrieval with generative AI by retrieving relevant documents before generating a response.
A search approach that retrieves information based on meaning, context, and user intent rather than exact keyword matches.
A search technique that finds similar content by comparing vector representations of data rather than matching exact text.
Numerical representations of text, images, or other content that capture meaning and allow similarity comparisons.
Structured networks of entities and relationships that represent knowledge and enable discovery, reasoning, and linked data applications.
A method of publishing structured data using shared identifiers and relationships so that information can be connected across systems.
The process of automatically adding related terms, synonyms, or concepts to a search query to improve retrieval results.
Natural Language Processing is a field of AI focused on enabling computers to understand, analyze, generate, and interact with human language.
A field of AI focused on enabling computers to analyze, interpret, generate, and interact with human language.
A technique that identifies and extracts entities such as people, organizations, places, dates, and events from text.
The process of assigning predefined categories or labels to documents, records, or other text-based content.
A method for identifying themes or topics that appear across a collection of documents.
The process of analyzing text to determine opinions, emotions, attitudes, or sentiment expressed by the author.
Technology that automatically translates text or speech from one language to another.
The process of generating shorter versions of longer documents while preserving key information and meaning.
The automated identification and collection of descriptive information from digital resources.
The automated creation of descriptive metadata such as titles, keywords, abstracts, and subject terms.
The process of determining when different records or references represent the same real-world entity.
The assignment of resources, documents, or records into categories based on their characteristics or content.
The identification and removal or consolidation of duplicate records within a dataset or catalog.
Optical Character Recognition converts images of printed text into machine-readable and searchable text.
A field of AI that enables computers to interpret, analyze, and understand visual information from images and video.
Technology that identifies objects, people, locations, or concepts within images.
Technology that converts handwritten text into machine-readable text.
A computer vision technique that identifies and locates specific objects within an image or video.
Technology that converts spoken language into text.
Technology that generates spoken language from text.
The automated conversion of spoken audio into written text.
Technology that reads written text aloud using synthetic speech.
Algorithms that suggest relevant resources, products, services, or content based on user behavior or item characteristics.
The use of historical data and machine learning models to forecast future events, behaviors, or trends.
A predictive technique used to estimate future demand, usage, or outcomes based on historical patterns.
The process of discovering patterns, relationships, and insights within large datasets.
The ability of an AI system to identify recurring structures, relationships, or trends within data.
The ability for people to find, reach, and use library resources or services.
The management of policies, staffing, budgets, and day-to-day operations.
AI that can plan and choose actions, often using tools, with a degree of autonomy.
Autonomous or semi-autonomous software components that act on behalf of a user or workflow.
Using data to understand usage, patterns, and outcomes.
The stewardship of historical records and materials for long-term preservation and access.
The process of evaluating quality, impact, or effectiveness.
Using systems to perform routine tasks with limited manual intervention.
The work of creating and maintaining records that describe and organize resources.
Conversational interfaces that answer questions through text or voice.
Networks of linked citations that show how scholarship relates.
Standard references that identify a source and where it was published.
Selecting, acquiring, and maintaining materials for a collection.
The ongoing care, review, and adjustment of a collection.
Selecting, organizing, and shaping content for a purpose or audience.
Support interactions that help users solve problems or access services.
Organizing, protecting, and maintaining data across its lifecycle.
Tools that help people make informed choices without replacing judgment.
Text or metadata that explains what a resource is about.
Digitized or born-digital materials organized for access and stewardship.
The process of converting physical materials into digital form.
The search and retrieval experience that helps people find resources.
The presence of different perspectives, identities, communities, or materials.
Written guidance that explains a system, process, policy, or resource.
Use cases that are being piloted or explored but are not yet routine.
Fair access, treatment, and outcomes across different user groups.
Responses from users or staff that help improve a service.
Financial support that enables a service, project, or infrastructure.
The examination of images to identify features, objects, or patterns.
Practices that make services and collections welcoming and usable for more people.
The ability to find, evaluate, and use information effectively.
Teaching or guided learning that builds user skills.
Help that assists teaching, training, or independent learning.
Shared rules or practices that guide library work and interoperability.
Machine Learning; systems that learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions.
A combined label for applications that use both machine learning and natural language processing.
A branch of AI where systems learn patterns from data rather than explicit rules.
Structured group conversations used to plan, decide, or coordinate work.
Structured information that describes, identifies, or manages a resource.
Services designed to support users across multiple languages.
A combined label for language applications that mix NLP methods with generative models.
Clear, direct writing intended to be easy for a broad audience to understand.
Setting goals, priorities, and steps before acting.
A rule or guiding principle that shapes decisions and behavior.
How much useful work can be completed with the available time and tools.
Library events, workshops, exhibits, or activities planned for a community or learning goal.
The process of buying or licensing tools, services, or materials.
Review steps that catch errors, inconsistencies, or defects.
Library guidance that helps readers find books or media they might enjoy.
Support that helps people read or interpret text.
Help that makes reading easier for specific users or contexts.
Suggestions for resources, actions, or content based on some criteria.
Library services that help users find, evaluate, and use information.
The production of summaries or outputs that communicate data or activity.
Systems that store and provide access to digital scholarly or institutional content.
Data produced, collected, or used as part of research activity.
The assessment of research quality, impact, or influence.
Services that help people plan, conduct, manage, or share research.
The creation, sharing, and preservation of research and scholarship.
The act of looking for information or the system that supports that task.
Choosing materials, tools, or content based on criteria.
AI systems that work with spoken language, including recognition, transcription, and synthesis.
Numerical information or the methods used to analyze it.
Converting audio, speech, or handwritten text into written text.
Converting text or speech from one language to another.
Information about how often and how heavily a service or resource is used.
How a person perceives and interacts with a service or system.
Direct support services that help patrons use the library.
Help with drafting, revising, or formatting written text.
